items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
items.correct: They are carried in the bloodstream and act on cells with specific receptors for that hormone.
items.misconception: Hormones are sent through nerves directly to specific organs.
items.random_wrong: Hormones are released directly into target organs by being injected by glands.
items.why: Students conflate nervous and endocrine signalling; they think every signal in the body travels through nerves.
The answer examiners want is: LH. “FSH” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Increase, so more water is reabsorbed
items.misconception: Decrease, so more water is reabsorbed
items.random_wrong: Increase, so urine becomes more dilute
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Increase, so more water is reabsorbed. “Decrease, so more water is reabsorbed” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a 28-day cycle, ovulation occurs around day:
items.why: The real figure is 14. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
